speaker: interviewer
text: This is Tape number 2. Today's date is August the 16th, 1996. The survivor's name is Jack Gun. You were talking about going to the Rozyszcze ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. [SIGHS] So I started to tell you, they put in sometimes three, four families in one little house. We were probably all of us-- the five of my family were in one little room. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Conditions in the-- the living conditions were very, very bad. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The sanitation was very bad. Lot of people got sick. Food was very scarce.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much food would they give you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They used to ration out so much each morning or each evening. They would-- you'd stand in line and they give you so much bread and a little bit of hot soup. And this is what you were supposed to survive on. But fortunately, my parents went out to work and there they somehow through a good friend, a farmer, that we had, used to sneak some food in to them. So when they used to come home at night, they used to bring a little extra food, little bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where-- where did your parents work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father worked in a-- in a place, they called it a [NON-ENGLISH] My father and brother, they used to work in a place where all the-- they used to-- the people used to bring in their tax for the German government. Whoever had cows used to bring a cow so often. And whoever had chickens used to bring chickens. Whoever was raising wheat used to bring wheat. That was their obligation to pay to the German government. And my father and brother used to work on this place where all this stuff was coming in. And-- and they used to take care of the cattle. And whatever that needed to be done, whatever dirty work there was to be done, that's what the Jews used to do. And my mother, I'm not too sure where she used to work. It could have been a laundry for the German soldiers. But she used to go out also. And they used to come back in the evening. And my sister and I and some more kids-- and there were a few-- there was an older woman also in the house. And she used to stay. And we used to stay in the house-- we're afraid to go outside, naturally-- and look out the window occasionally. And occasionally you'd see a German soldier or someone hitting an old person with a butt of a gun.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember seeing that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it ever anyone-- anyone that you knew that was being hit?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody I knew personally. And so we were naturally sitting there scared, with fear. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what you used to talk about with her, what kinds of things you would discuss?

speaker: interviewee
text: With who?

speaker: interviewer
text: With your sister, when your parents were away, when your brother was gone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. And you know what else? My sister for a long time-- I want to backtrack a little bit. My father used to deal with a lot of Gentiles naturally, when he heard the stores and that. And he got very befriended with a Czech. We had-- near Rozyszcze, there was a town called-- a village called Uzowa. And in Uzowa, there were about approximately maybe 12, 15 Czech [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Gentiles, naturally, that had farms, quite nice-size farms. And they were very prosperous farmers. And one of them used to do an awful lot of business with my father, you know, used to buy all his-- the material to make clothes for the family and linens and whatever. And my father was very, very befriended with him. And my father just before the war, just before the Germans came in, he gave them away everything we had, a lot of stuff from the store, you know, a lot of fabrics and a lot of, I'm sure, jewelry and maybe the fur coats. Whatever we possessed almost he gave to this one Czech. And he told him, he says, look, he says, you know the Germans are coming in. He says, and you know what might happen to Jews, we don't know. We want you to keep all this for me. And I'm hoping, he says, in case we need it, that you will help us one day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's what happened. So this Czech-- What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Mr. Yarushka. And so this Czech used to help out a lot-- used to sneak-- he came in once to the ghetto and took my sister out. My sister-- most of us were blond, blue-eye. We didn't resemble Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he took my sister out and took him-- took her to his farm. And she was there working, helping him out. And-- and she had it a lot better, naturally, than being in a ghetto. So she was away for quite a while while we were in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: And just-- just before we're-- just before the ghetto was liquidated, just when the time where she shouldn't have been there, she got very, very homesick and asks this Mr. Yarushka, she says, you know, I miss my parents so much and I'm missing my brothers and I'd like to go back. So I mean, he didn't want to discourage her, which I wish he would of, and she came back to the ghetto, which was a terrible, terrible mistake. So this is why I don't remember being too much with my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what--what you did when you were-- were you by yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, with some more kids and there were some older women that didn't go to work. And what did I do? I really don't know. I was just sitting in a corner, peaking out a window at times. And that's it. That was my recreation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other kids that you-- who were from your town that were there too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They were from my town, the kids. But I really-- you know, I-- I don't know. We had nothing to-- you know, we had no toys. We had nothing to-- we had no crayons. We had no-- we had nothing actually to entertain ourselves. Maybe we were playing tic-tac-toe. I don't know. I-- I really don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- could you describe what the ghetto looked like, how large it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: How large? Well, it was a-- one big street with little dilapidated houses. And they put a gate in the front. And they had barbed wire around, to make sure that nobody goes out when they're not supposed to. This is what it looked like. And my brother tells me that every so often-- you see, they had also Jewish police in the ghetto. The Germans, you know, tried to make it easy on themself, so they had-- they took Jewish people and made them policemen. And they said they were supposed to watch the gates. And every so often they used to go to this Jewish police and tell them that, you know, we need to get so much gold for this week. So the Jewish police used to come around to every little house and say, hey, we need to collect so much and so much gold, or else they're going to kill us. So they used to come and ask for gold. Whoever had I guess gave it to them and whoever didn't, they didn't, or else they'd come another week and they'd say, we need so many fur coats for the German government. And I mean, most of the stuff the people left right in their houses or they gave it away before. Whatever we could take to the ghetto was really only personal, a little bit of underwear maybe. My mother had me carry, I believe, a pillow, just to make sure I have someplace to put my head on, being a little boy. So most of the people didn't have much possessions with them anymore. Maybe a few did. And they used to harass-- harass the people constantly, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was anything done to people that couldn't give anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: They used to-- you know, what they used to do, I understand they used to take out sometimes a few people and take them away and I don't know what happened to them. Or they killed them or they took them to another labor camp someplace nearby. Things like this they did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any people on the police, Jews that you knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: I personally, me, I was too young to know. But my father and brother knew. In fact, they wanted my father to be one the head people from the police there. And my father refused. He said, I used to do things, you know, a lot of duties for the-- for people before the war. He says, I don't want to be involved in this.

speaker: interviewer
text: So do you remember what-- the other families that were also in the rooms together?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my little house?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't. I really don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what it was like living with everyone all together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I remember, you know, being cuddled up in one little corner, you know, no room to move. And we had very little clothes, very little food. Like I said before, the sanitation conditions were very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were they like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very bad. There was hardly any water to wash.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there bathrooms?

speaker: interviewee
text: Outside. Outdoor bathrooms. And they-- you know, before-- before the war, they used to clean them and, you know, take out the sewage. This-- now everything was piling up and things were, you know-- people that weren't strong and healthy, a lot of them just died during the year of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see any Germans inside the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Sure. There were German soldiers, Ukrainian soldiers, and Jewish police. But the Jewish police didn't have-- you know, they were just like-- [SIGHS] the Germans promised them a little extra. They said, we're not going to do anything to you, so they, you know-- they thought that they would save themselves by being police. But at the end, they did them the same as they did everybody else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- how were you able to differentiate between a Ukrainian and a German soldier?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, they wore different uniform, I believe. The Ukrainians were wearing some kind of brown. And the Germans was more of a greenish-- dark green.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were your parents working for a majority of the day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They left early in the morning, came back at night. And this was going on for about a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are there any particular things that stand out for you in the ghetto, any memories, any days or incidents or something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, mainly the incident that I saw a soldier hitting somebody in the head with the butt of the gun and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a reason? Do you know if they-- would they just hit people randomly?

speaker: interviewee
text: The main reason? No, just-- the reason was he was Jewish. They didn't like the way he walked or the way he looked. That was the main reason. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Not because he didn't obey them, because they sure did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so when was it-- was the-- was this ghetto eventually liquidated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was. The ghetto was liquidated in August, August the 22nd, to be exact, 1942. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And what happened was there started to be rumors in town, in the ghetto, you know. And we heard already in the beginning of August or in the middle of August, my-- my father heard that they were going to do something. They didn't know what. They didn't know if they going to take them to the labor camps or they're going to ship them to Poland or wherever. But there were rumors that the ghetto was going to be liquidated.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father made a plan. And he-- he was planning to take-- sneak me out with him and my brother when they were going out to work this one morning. And my mother was supposed to sneak out my sister. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And at the end of the day, instead of coming back to the ghetto, we were all supposed to meet at this Mr. Yarushka's place. My mother knew where he was, and naturally my father knew exactly where it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were supposed to meet there and see what we can do, if he would hide us or he would-- somehow run away someplace. We knew that the ghetto-- they knew that something is-- drastic is going to happen very shortly. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the rumor mill worked and it worked right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So on August the 21st, 1942, that morning my father and my brother took me along. And somehow they snuck me out, brought me over to the [NON-ENGLISH where they worked and took me into a barn with hay and left me there. And during the day, they would-- one of them would come and check on me occasionally and bring me a piece of bread whenever they got their meals or whatever. And I laid there all alone. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: During the day, somebody came-- came by to that place where my father and brother were working and told my brothers-- my-- told my father, he says, you know, Shmuel, I want you to know that your wife and daughter were held up by the gate. They were not allowed out to work. So naturally, he was surprised and disappointed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the day ended, when they were ready to go back to the ghetto, my father came over to where I was and brought my brother. And he said, look, he says, I want you two guys sleep here. Don't go back to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, I will not leave your mother and your sister alone in the ghetto. He says, I'm going back to the ghetto. And if tomorrow-- if there is a tomorrow, he says hopefully that they'll be able to come out and we'll meet the following day at Mr. Yarushka's. But the tomorrow never came, because that was-- this was the day, August the 22nd. They didn't let anybody out of the-- out of the ghetto. They took approximately 4,000 Jews which were in the ghetto, put them on trucks, took them about 15, 20 kilometers out of-- out of Rozyszcze. They had-- they had ditches dug already. And surprisingly, the ditches were dug not by Jews, but by Ukrainians, because they didn't want the Jews to get any kind of idea, so, you know, panic. So they had the ditches dug. They put everybody in and machine-gunned them to death. And whoever wasn't dead from the bullets, they died after they covered them up. And this was the end of the Rozyszcze ghetto. Meantime, my brother and I laid there. In the morning, we noticed our father didn't come. And the first thing that we heard in the morning, we heard German soldiers coming in and hollering, Juden, any Juden, Juden raus, any Jews, come on out. And we were laying there in the hay. And we could-- we heard them, you know, they were jabbing with bayonets, trying to see if there's anybody there. And we just laid there numb. And luckily, they didn't find us. We laid there the whole day. We could hear a lot of commotion. This was very close to the main highway, and we-- we could hear trucks running back and forth, trucks, noises, hollering. But naturally, we didn't know what happened. But we knew something had happened, because otherwise our father would have been back there in the morning, to work. And we laid there 'til probably midnight, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] maybe longer. I don't know exactly the time. And then we walked out. And as we walked out, we walked maybe-- I'm not sure. When we walked out, you know, with the fear-- the fear was so great that if we heard anything-- we heard a leaf fall, we were scared. We heard a dog bark, you know, figured they're after us. But we didn't walk too far. We walked maybe a half a kilometer. And my brother knew there was a man there had a little house and a barn. So he knew who the man was. You know, he didn't know him well, but he knew of him. And he saw there was a ladder standing, you know, going up to an attic in the barn. So we climbed up to the-- into that attic, myself and my brother. And when we came up to the attic, there were two other Jewish guys laying there, also that used to work there, in that same [NON-ENGLISH]. And the four of us laid there. And the following morning, the man came in, I guess to-- to check on this cattle or whatever he had there. And I guess he heard us moving, because, you know, we laid in a very, very tight spot. We couldn't-- we couldn't sit up. We were the second day without water, without food, It was August. It was hot, and you know, laying in a little cubbyhole, you know, and the sun was beating.

speaker: interviewer
text: But anyway, that man heard us and he climbed up the ladder. He took a look. He says, oh my gosh. He says, what you guys doing here? How did you get here?

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, you know they took all the Jews out of your-- out of Rozyszcze and they killed them. He says, how did you-- Anyway, he says, look. He says, I'll tell you. He says, I'm going to go down. I can see you guys are tired and you look all-- he says, I'm going to bring you some water. He says, I'm going to bring you some bread. And he says, I'll tell you I'll let you stay here until nightfall. He says, when it gets dark, I want you to leave, because I cannot for-- I cannot keep you here. He says, the Germans put out bulletins saying that if they find any Gentile harboring a Jew, they'll kill the Gentile the same as the Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he says, I won't-- he says, I won't tell anybody you're here. But soon as it gets night, you guys leave. So he did bring us up the water-- and naturally that was a great help-- and a little bread. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we-- made us feel an awful lot better for a few minutes, anyway.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when it came nightfall, my brother decided that he wants to go to this Mr. Yarushka. But my brother, you know, was-- naturally he was also a young fella. He knew of him and knew approximately where he was, but he didn't know the roads. And he asked this one man there, which was quite a bit older-- he was maybe in his 40s. And he asked him, he says, tell me, Shloime, he says, how do I get to Uzowa, and to this and-- and this man knew the-- used to-- I think he used to ride horses or sell horses and he knew all the villages and everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he explained to him exactly how to go. So my brother and I walked down when it got night and we went. And the other two remained there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: We walked. We started to walk, you know. This I can remember, you know, the-- it was like in the stillness of the night, you know. First of all, it was a small town, naturally. But everything was so quiet. And every time we heard anything, you know, we sort of fell on the ground and laid down. We heard a dog, we heard any-- any movement, we felt it was they're coming after us. And it was quite-- it was quite a long walk. I don't know exactly how far it was. It was approximately 15 kilometers and-- and me being a little fella and didn't have much food for the last few days and I was hungry and thirsty and tired, and so I used to tell my brother, I can't walk any farther. So he used to take me on his shoulder and carry me for a little bit and then let me down and then again. Finally, when we arrived to Mr. Yarushka's it was probably early morning, maybe 1:00, 2 o'clock, I don't know. And my brother knocked on the door and Mr. Yarushka came out. And when he took a look at us, he crossed himself.

speaker: interviewer
text: He says, my God. He says, how did you guys get away? He says, you know, they killed everybody. so we told them we heard. And we told him the story, you know, how we stayed in that barn and that. And he asked my brother, he says, tell me, he says-- he says, how did you know exactly how to find my house? So my brother told him. He says, you know, I was with this and this man and I asked them how to get to you, which was probably a big mistake, because, you know, he felt that if somebody else knows that we're going to his, house-- so he was sort of-- he was scared of keeping us also.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he told us, he says, look. He says, I'm going to do whatever I can to help you two boys. But, he says, I cannot keep you on my property, he says, because the Germans gave out this Ordnung that anybody that harbors Jews, helps Jews, they're going to kill their families the same as they killed a Jew. And you know, you can blame him. I-- you-- now I realize that I couldn't blame him for it, but this was it. He says, I'll tell you what you guys do. He says, I'll see so you have food. He says, right now, he says, we-- the wheat is high. He says, why don't you guys go and lay in the wheat. And at night, he says, you come into the house. And we'll give you a little food. Maybe you'll wash. He says, and some nights I'll bring it out to you, he says, not to make it obvious so anybody sees you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is what we started to do, end of August. And we were lying all day in the wheat. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And one incident that happened to me laying in the wheat was that I-- I got the sunstroke. I had nothing to protect my head, I guess.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was very fair complect and I got a sunstroke. And I didn't make any move. I didn't react at all. And my brother talked to me and he got very scared.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he went back to the-- at night, he went to Mr. Yarushka and he said, you know, I think something happened to my brother. He doesn't move. He lays there and he looks like he's dead.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, well, maybe he got some sun-- too much sun, he says. Why don't you take some milk and some honey. He says, try to open his mouth, he says, and give him a little of the milk and honey. He says, see if that will help. And sure enough, that was the right medication. He brought that and he kept opening my mouth and he kept putting it in. And finally after 10 minutes or so, I reacted and started to move and started to talk. So that was one of the first incidents that happened to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how long you were in that condition for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, probably for like a day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the-- the heat of the sun?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Sure do. The heat-- it was hot, very hot. And I don't think we had-- we must have had a little bit of water or I don't know how we carried it. There was no Thermos then where you filled up a Thermos with ice. But we had very little to drink, had a little bit of food, you know. But during the day, you know, the-- with the sun heating, the thirst was terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: And would-- you were just lying in the field the whole day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just lying. Couldn't-- you can't raise up. If you raise up, somebody might see you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to talk to your brother at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Used to whisper to each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what kinds of things you would talk about?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS]. Well, not really. I probably could-- he tells me that I used to ask him what happened to my parents and-- and to my sister and-- I mean, naturally, all he could tell me was, you know, maybe they ran away too and maybe we'll get together with them. And you know, he gave me hope. I'm sure that he knew different. I'm sure he also-- you know, when there's life, there's hope, so we were-- he was hoping. But most likely, he knew what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you-- you had trust in him that what he-- that he had more knowledge about the situation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I had to have trust in him, because he was my-- he was like my father at the time, my protector. And he was all I had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just to backtrack a little, do you remember when your father had left the day that he had gone out to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Back to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewer
text: --back in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I recall it. But you know, all of it I recall as a dream, you know. It's-- being so young and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I just remember everything, you know, so vaguely. But I do remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the end of Tape number 2.
